composerData.name: Implementation of PLAN.md

nodes.label: generalPurpose
nodes.inlineContent: - generalPurpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. Use when searching for a keyword or file and not confident you'll find the match quickly.

nodes.label: explore
nodes.inlineContent: - explore: Fast, readonly agent specialized for exploring codebases. Use this when you need to quickly find files by patterns (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), search code for keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer questions about the codebase (eg. "how do API endpoints work?"). This agent operates in read-only mode and cannot modify files. When calling this agent, specify the desired thoroughness level: "quick" for basic searches, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" for comprehensive analysis across multiple locations and naming conventions.

nodes.label: shell
nodes.inlineContent: - shell: Command execution specialist for running bash commands. Use this for git operations, command execution, and other terminal tasks.

nodes.label: browser-use
nodes.inlineContent: - browser-use: Perform browser-based testing and web automation. This subagent can navigate web pages, interact with elements, fill forms, and take screenshots. Use this for testing web applications, verifying UI changes, or any browser-based tasks. Use this browser subagent when you need to either: (1) parallelize browser tasks alongside other work, or (2) execute a longer sequence of browser actions that benefit from dedicated context. For simple, single browser actions, you may use the browser tools directly. This subagent requires agent mode because browser MCP access is unavailable in readonly mode. This subagent_type is stateful; if a browserUse subagent already exists, the previously created subagent will be resumed if you reuse the Task tool with subagent_type set to browserUse. (Auto-resumes most recent agent of this type; `resume` arg is ignored)

nodes.label: cursor-guide
nodes.inlineContent: - cursor-guide: Read Cursor product documentation to answer questions about how Cursor Desktop, IDE, CLI, Cloud Agents, Bugbot, and other features work. Use when the user asks 'In Cursor, how do I...?' or similar questions about Cursor products.

nodes.label: ci-investigator
nodes.inlineContent: - ci-investigator: Investigate a single failing PR CI check and return a short root-cause summary. Use when the user asks to summarize, explain, diagnose, or investigate a specific failed check from a pull request.

nodes.label: bugbot
nodes.inlineContent:
- bugbot: Use only when the user *explicitly* asks for a Bugbot-like review of local code changes. When launching this subagent, set the Task description to exactly "Bugbot". Launch exactly one Bugbot subagent with `readonly: true` and `run_in_background: false` unless the user explicitly asks to run in background. Use this fixed prompt form: "Full Repository Path: ...
Diff: <one of: "branch changes", "uncommitted changes", "natural language">
Change Description: ...
Custom Instructions: ..."; default to `Diff: branch changes`; include `Change Description` only when `Diff` is `natural language`, formatting it as one block per changed file (a `<path> (added|modified|deleted|renamed)` header followed by bullets of what changed, mentioning line numbers or ranges inline where helpful), and only use `natural language` as a last resort after a regular diff-based review failed because the diff could not be computed; include `Custom Instructions` only when the user gave specific review instructions. This subagent is single-shot and does not support `resume`; always launch a fresh subagent instead.

nodes.label: security-review
nodes.inlineContent: - security-review: Use only when the user *explicitly* asks for a security review of local code changes. When launching this subagent, set the Task description to exactly "Security Review". Launch exactly one security-review subagent with `readonly: true` and `run_in_background: false` unless the user explicitly asks to run in background. Use this fixed prompt form: "Full Repository Path: ...\nDiff: <one of: \"branch changes\", \"uncommitted changes\">\nCustom Instructions: ..."; default to `Diff: branch changes`; include `Custom Instructions` only when the user gave specific review instructions. This subagent is single-shot and does not support `resume`; always launch a fresh subagent instead.

nodes.label: best-of-n-runner
nodes.inlineContent: - best-of-n-runner: Run a task in an isolated git worktree. Each best-of-n-runner gets its own branch and working directory. Use for best-of-N parallel attempts or isolated experiments.

todos.content: Inspect data schemas and app requirements
Add time controls, filters, search, details, focus, deep links
Install dependencies and verify production build

toolFormerData.name: read_file_v2
toolFormerData.result:
{"contents":"# PLAN.md

You are building a website from scratch. This file is your **only** instruction —
there will be no follow-up questions or clarifications. Read it fully, make
reasonable decisions where it leaves room, and ship something complete.

## What to build

An **interactive 3D visualization of the inner solar system and its near-Earth
asteroids**, driven entirely by the dataset in `data/`.

The dataset describes the Sun, the eight planets, and ~42,000 near-Earth objects
as **orbital elements** (see `data/README.md` for the schema, units, and frame).
Positions are *not* given — you compute where each body is from its orbit, for
any moment in time, and let the user move through time to watch the system
evolve.

This has a required **base** (below) that every submission must get right, and a
set of **open-ended directions** on top. The base is the foundation; the
directions are where you bring your own ideas. Build a solid base, then add as
much meaningful capability on top as you can.

You may search the internet to answer questions you have, or to gather assets.

## Required base

1. **Render the solar system in 3D.** The Sun at the center, the eight planets,
   and the near-Earth asteroids, positioned by computing each body's location
   from its orbital elements (propagated from each body's own epoch). Orbits
   should be geometrically correct — a viewer who knows the solar system should
   recognize it.
2. **Time is interactive.** The scene must show a specific moment and let the
   user advance/rewind time (e.g. play/pause, speed, scrub, or jump to a date),
   with bodies moving along their orbits accordingly.
3. **Use the real data.** Load it from `data/` at runtime via `fetch`. Handle the
   full ~42,000-object set — performance and how you present that many objects
   are part of getting the base right. Do not hardcode or fabricate positions.
   You may pre-process data as part of the build to improve performance.
4. **It must be usable and look intentional.** Camera control, a sense of scale,
   and legible presentation. This is a tool people explore, not a tech demo.

## Open-ended directions

Implement each of these additional features — the
approach, depth, and UX are yours to design. A few directions to consider:

- **Filter & search** the asteroids (e.g. by hazard status, size, orbit class,
  distance, approach date — whatever the data supports).
- **Investigate a single object** — a detail view surfacing its orbit, physical
  properties, and close-approach history (`close-approaches.json`).
- **Highlight what matters** — e.g. potentially hazardous asteroids, upcoming
  close approaches, or unusually large/close objects.
- **Convey scale and risk** through visual encoding.
- **Add comets** — overlay the comets in `comets.json` (same orbital-element
  schema as the asteroids). Note that many comets have highly eccentric or open
  (hyperbolic/parabolic) orbits, which are not propagated the same way as the
  asteroids' closed orbits.
- **Show impact risk** — overlay the CNEOS impact-risk data in `sentry.json`
  (joinable to objects by designation): impact probability, Palermo/Torino scale,
  and the window of potential impacts.
- **Focus & follow** — let the user lock the camera onto a selected body and
  track it as time advances.
- **Shareable deep links** — encode the current view (time, selected object,
  camera) in the URL so a particular moment can be linked and reopened.

If you think you cannot accomplish any of the above or you cannot make it work, do not complete it 
or leave a partially completed but working feature.

## Hard rules (graded)

1. **Do not modify, move, or delete anything in `data/`.** Treat it as read-only.
2. **No external network calls.** Everything the site needs must come from `data/`
   and your own bundle. No CDNs, remote fonts, or runtime API calls.
3. **`pnpm install && pnpm build` must succeed** from this directory with no
   manual steps, and emit a self-contained static site into **`dist/`**. 
   This should also contain a copy of the data in the format you need it in for the UI.
4. The built site is served from a sub-path inside an `<iframe>`, so use
   **relative** asset and data URLs (not absolute `/…` paths).
5. Keep everything inside this directory. Do not read or write files outside it.

## Deliverable structure

```
./                 # your project root (this directory)
├── PLAN.md         # this file — leave it in place
├── data/           # provided input — READ ONLY
├── src/<source files>  # your application source
└── dist/           # output of `pnpm build` — the compiled static site
```

You may use any framework, libraries (bundled, not from a CDN). If you
make notable assumptions, jot them in a `README.md`.
","totalLinesInFile":93}

toolFormerData.name: read_file_v2
toolFormerData.result:
{"contents":"# Benchmark Data

A fixed snapshot of NASA/JPL small-body and planetary data. **Read-only** — do
not modify, move, or delete anything in this directory. Load it at runtime with
`fetch` (relative paths); make no external network calls.

Provenance and exact query URLs are in [`provenance.json`](./provenance.json).
The snapshot is regenerated by `scripts/fetch-data.mjs` + `scripts/build-planets.mjs`.

## Files

| file | what | rows |
|---|---|---|
| `asteroids.json` | All known Near-Earth Objects (NEOs) with orbital + physical data | ~42,000 |
| `close-approaches.json` | NEO close-approach events to Earth | ~50,000 |
| `planets.json` | The eight planets' orbital elements + radii | 8 |
| `comets.json` | Comets, same element schema (optional overlay) | ~4,000 |
| `sentry.json` | CNEOS impact-risk summary (optional overlay) | ~2,200 |
| `provenance.json` | Sources, query URLs, retrieval timestamp | — |

All three data files are **arrays of objects**. Values are numbers or `null`
(many physical fields are unknown for less-studied objects).

## Coordinate & element conventions

Orbital elements describe a heliocentric orbit in the **J2000 ecliptic** frame.
Angles are in **degrees**, distances in **astronomical units (au)**, time in
**days**. Each object carries its **own** `epoch` (a Julian date) — propagate
each body from its own epoch.

Positions are **not** stored; they must be **computed** from the elements for a
chosen time (i.e. solve the orbit / Kepler's equation and advance the mean
anomaly by the mean motion). The Sun is the central body, at the origin, with a
mean radius of **696,000 km**.

### Orbital element fields (in `asteroids.json` and `planets.json`)

| field | meaning | unit |
|---|---|---|
| `a` | semi-major axis | au |
| `e` | eccentricity | — |
| `i` | inclination | deg |
| `om` | longitude of ascending node (Ω) | deg |
| `w` | argument of perihelion (ω) | deg |
| `ma` | mean anomaly at epoch | deg |
| `epoch` | epoch of the elements | Julian date |
| `n` | mean motion (deg/day) — present on planets; derivable elsewhere | deg/day |
| `per` | orbital period | days |
| `q`, `ad` | perihelion / aphelion distance | au |
| `tp` | time of perihelion passage | Julian date |

### `asteroids.json` — additional fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `full_name`, `pdes`, `name` | display name, primary designation, IAU name (may be null) |
| `spkid` | unique JPL SPK-ID |
| `neo`, `pha` | booleans: Near-Earth Object / Potentially Hazardous Asteroid |
| `class` | orbit class code (e.g. `APO`, `ATE`, `AMO`, `IEO`) |
| `moid` | minimum orbit intersection distance with Earth (au) |
| `H` | absolute magnitude (brightness) |
| `G` | magnitude slope parameter |
| `diameter` | estimated diameter (km) — often null |
| `albedo` | geometric albedo — often null |
| `rot_per` | rotation period (hours) — often null |
| `spec_B`, `spec_T` | spectral type (SMASSII / Tholen) — often null |
| `first_obs` | date of first observation |

### `planets.json` — additional fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `name` | planet name |
| `radius_km` | mean radius (km) |

### `comets.json` (optional overlay)

Comets, using the **same orbital-element fields** as asteroids (`a e i om w ma
epoch q per n tp`) plus `full_name`, `pdes`, `class`, `M1` (total magnitude), and
`diameter`. **Caution:** roughly half are hyperbolic or near-parabolic
(`e >= 1`) — these have no period/mean-motion and must be propagated from `tp`
(time of perihelion passage), not from `ma`/`n`.

### `sentry.json` (optional overlay)

CNEOS Sentry impact-risk summary, one row per monitored object. Join to asteroids
on `des` ↔ asteroid `pdes`.

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `des`, `fullname` | designation / display name |
| `ip` | cumulative impact probability |
| `ps_cum`, `ps_max` | cumulative / max Palermo scale |
| `ts_max` | max Torino scale (0–10) |
| `range` | year range of potential impacts |
| `n_imp` | number of potential impacts |
| `diameter`, `h`, `v_inf` | size (km), abs. magnitude, encounter velocity (km/s) |
| `last_obs`, `last_obs_jd` | last observation date |

### `close-approaches.json`

One row per Earth close-approach event. Join to asteroids on
`des` ↔ asteroid `pdes`.

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `des` | object designation (matches asteroid `pdes`) |
| `cd` | close-approach date/time (calendar, TDB) |
| `jd` | close-approach time (Julian date) |
| `dist` | nominal approach distance (au) |
| `dist_min`, `dist_max` | 3σ distance bounds (au) |
| `v_rel`, `v_inf` | relative / unperturbed velocity (km/s) |
| `h` | absolute magnitude of the object |
","totalLinesInFile":114}

thinking.text:
**Planning implementation details**

I need to implement a project, possibly using Vite with vanilla TypeScript and Three.js. First, I'll create package files, maybe without using Git, and install what’s needed, starting with scaffolding. It seems the user might prefer pnpm, so I need to create relevant files like package.json and tsconfig. 

Vite will serve from the public directory in production, but I also need a copy script to handle data after the build. Dependencies will need to be considered as well.
**Exploring orbital implementations**

I’m considering using Three.js along with Vite for a project that focuses on vanilla TypeScript, possibly without React. I need to implement user interfaces and orbital math, including elliptical and hyperbolic calculations for comets. The directions mention avoiding partial implementations, so even if I can't get hyperbolic working, I should still focus on elliptical orbits.

I’ll calculate orbit positions using formulas for mean anomaly and apply the appropriate transformations. For hyperbolic cases, I want to address the equations, keeping in mind the necessary units and methods for solving them.
**Exploring point visualization**

I'm considering using instanced points with visual features like the OrbitControls from three.js. For UI overlays, should I implement a search/filter for subsets? The Points geometry can include all items but hide some via alpha or size adjustments. I might utilize a custom ShaderMaterial for per-point attributes like color and size. Perhaps organizing items into categories, like all asteroids and those marked as PHAs, will help. I should also manage visible positions for filtered subsets efficiently.

I’m thinking about generating samples for both closed elliptical and possibly hyperbolic paths. lines.content: export function parseDateInput(value: string): number | null {
export function positionFromElements(elements: OrbitElements, jd: number): Vec3 | null {
if (e === null || inc === null || om === null || w === null) {
return positionElliptic(elements, jd, e, inc, om, w);
return rotatePerifocal(positionParabolic(q, jd - tp), inc, om, w);
return rotatePerifocal(positionHyperbolic(q, e, jd - tp), inc, om, w);
export function orbitPath(elements: OrbitElements, samples = 256): Vec3[] {
if (e === null || inc === null || om === null || w === null) {
const y = a * Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, 1 - e * e)) * Math.sin(eccentricAnomaly);
const maxTrueAnomaly = e > 1 ? Math.acos(-1 / e) * 0.88 : Math.PI * 0.82;
const r = e === 1 ? (2 * q) / (1 + Math.cos(nu)) : (q * (1 + e)) / (1 + e * Math.cos(nu));
points.push(rotatePerifocal([r * Math.cos(nu), r * Math.sin(nu), 0], inc, om, w));
if (a === null || ma === null || epoch === null || a <= 0) {
const meanMotionDeg = finite(elements.n) ?? (Math.sqrt(MU / (a * a * a)) / DEG_TO_RAD);
const meanAnomaly = normalizeRadians((ma + meanMotionDeg * (jd - epoch)) * DEG_TO_RAD);
const y = a * Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, 1 - e * e)) * Math.sin(eccentricAnomaly);
return rotatePerifocal([x, y, 0], inc, om, w);
function positionParabolic(q: number, daysSincePerihelion: number): Vec3 {
const scale = Math.sqrt((2 * q * q * q) / MU);
const f = d + (d * d * d) / 3 - target;
function positionHyperbolic(q: number, e: number, daysSincePerihelion: number): Vec3 {
const meanAnomaly = Math.sqrt(MU / (aAbs * aAbs * aAbs)) * daysSincePerihelion;
const y = aAbs * Math.sqrt(e * e - 1) * Math.sinh(h);
function solveKeplerElliptic(meanAnomaly: number, e: number): number {
function solveKeplerHyperbolic(meanAnomaly: number, e: number): number {
let h = sign * Math.log((2 * Math.abs(meanAnomaly)) / e + 1.8);
const delta = (e * Math.sinh(h) - h - meanAnomaly) / (e * Math.cosh(h) - 1);
function rotatePerifocal([x, y, z]: Vec3, incDeg: number, omDeg: number, wDeg: number): Vec3 {
const x1 = x * cosW - y * sinW;
const y1 = x * sinW + y * cosW;
const y2 = y1 * cosI - z1 * sinI;
const z2 = y1 * sinI + z1 * cosI;
const x3 = x2 * cosOm - y2 * sinOm;
const y3 = x2 * sinOm + y2 * cosOm;
function finite(value: number | null | undefined): number | null {
return typeof value === "number" && Number.isFinite(value) ? value : null;
import type { Asteroid, CloseApproach, Comet, LoadedData, Planet, SentryRisk } from "./types";

return { planets, asteroids, comets, sentryByDes, approachesByDes };

thinking.text:
**Adjusting raycaster and scene updates**

I'm working on raycaster meshes and points, needing to accurately distinguish them by setting `object.userData.kind`. I’ll find the selected position using the pointer coordinates from the camera.

For the scene update, I should adjust the positions of planets and asteroids, including a selected marker and orbit settings. I'm determining the time animation, setting up the slider range for a timeframe of ±36525 days. Also, I need to handle data fetching and renderer resizing, and manage how search results are displayed effectively.
**Considering raycast with bounding sphere**

I'm thinking about using a bounding sphere for the raycasting on points, and I’ll need to compute this after the update. It seems like a good approach to ensure accuracy in my selections. All positions are propagated from each object's own orbital epoch. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom, click a body to inspect it.

Click a planet, asteroid, or comet point to inspect orbit and risk data.

39273369-5016-467e-a906-b79b647c3943.text: The production build succeeds and copies the data into `dist/data/`. I’m adding a short `README.md` with assumptions and usage notes, then I’ll do one final build verification.

lines.content: Interactive 3D visualization of the planets, near-Earth asteroids, comets, close approaches, and CNEOS Sentry risk rows from the local `data/` snapshot.
The production build emits a static site into `dist/` and copies `data/` to `dist/data/`. Runtime fetches use relative URLs so the site can be served from a subpath or inside an iframe.
- Planet, asteroid, and comet positions are propagated from the orbital elements in `data/`; positions are not hardcoded.
- Elliptical orbits use mean anomaly and mean motion from each object's own epoch.
- Comet rendering includes elliptical, parabolic, and hyperbolic propagation from perihelion data when available.
- Planet radii and asteroid/comet point sizes are visually exaggerated so the system remains legible at astronomical scale.

8638fd7c-6754-40f6-9795-dfde6ebfe88d.text: The README is added. I’m running the required build command once more from a clean app state to confirm the final project still emits the static site correctly.

f1e3c35f-49f3-4bcb-8e0c-21c0e0981752.text:
Implemented `PLAN.md` as a complete Vite/Three.js static site.

Built features include 3D planet/NEO/comet rendering from orbital elements, interactive time controls, asteroid filtering/search, Sentry risk highlighting, close-approach details, object selection, focus/follow, and shareable URL state. The build copies `data/` into `dist/data/` and uses relative URLs.

Verification: `pnpm build` succeeds and emits `dist/`. Vite reports a non-fatal chunk-size warning for the bundled app JS.
